title: Apply: galaxies and gravity
description: Apply your understanding of galaxies and gravity in this set of free, standards-aligned practice questions.
topic_path: Middle school Earth and space science - NGSS

assessment_items.question_content:
The table below shows the masses of three satellites orbiting Earth. Assume that all three satellites are the same distance from the planet.

Satellite | Mass ($\text{kg}$)
:-: | :-:
A | $500$
B | $9{,}200$
C | $1{,}600$

**Which satellite experiences the strongest force of gravity from Earth?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The force of gravity between two objects depends on their masses and the distance between them. The greater the masses and the shorter the distance, the stronger the force of gravity.
In this case, all of the satellites are the same distance from Earth. So, we only need to compare their masses. According to the table, the satellite with the greatest mass is satellite B.
So, satellite B experiences the strongest force of gravity from Earth.

A | $2{,}100$
B | $400$
C | $6{,}700$

**Which satellite experiences the strongest force of gravity from Earth?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The force of gravity between two objects depends on their masses and the distance between them. The greater the masses and the shorter the distance, the stronger the force of gravity.
In this case, all of the satellites are the same distance from Earth. So, we only need to compare their masses. According to the table, the satellite with the greatest mass is satellite C.

assessment_items.question_content:
The table below shows the masses of three satellites orbiting Earth. Assume that all three satellites are the same distance from the planet.

Satellite | Mass ($\text{kg}$)
:-: | :-:
A | $8{,}900$
B | $1{,}500$
C | $300$

**Which satellite experiences the weakest force of gravity from Earth?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The force of gravity between two objects depends on their masses and the distance between them. The smaller the masses and the longer the distance, the weaker the force of gravity.
In this case, all of the satellites are the same distance from Earth. So, we only need to compare their masses. According to the table, the satellite with the smallest mass is satellite C.
So, satellite C experiences the weakest force of gravity from Earth.

assessment_items.question_content:
The Milky Way is surrounded by many smaller galaxies known as dwarf galaxies. The diagram below shows the relative distances of three dwarf galaxies from the Milky Way. All of the dwarf galaxies have roughly the same mass.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which dwarf galaxy experiences the strongest force of gravity from the Milky Way?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The force of gravity between two objects depends on their masses and the distance between them. The greater the masses and the shorter the distance, the stronger the force of gravity.
In this case, all of the dwarf galaxies have the same mass. So, we only need to consider distance. According to the diagram, the galaxy that is closest to the Milky Way is Ursa Minor.
So, Ursa Minor experiences the strongest force of gravity from the Milky Way.

options.alt: The Milky Way is shown surrounded by three smaller galaxies labeled Carina, Ursa Minor, and Draco. Carina is to the left of the Milky Way at the longest distance away. Ursa Minor is to the right of the Milky Way at the shortest distance away. Draco is to the right of the Milky Way at an intermediate distance away.

In this case, all of the dwarf galaxies have the same mass. So, we only need to consider distance. According to the diagram, the galaxy that is farthest from the Milky Way is Carina.
So, Carina experiences the weakest force of gravity from the Milky Way.

assessment_items.question_content:
Jace is using a computer simulation to measure the force of gravity between two objects. The simulation allows him to change the masses of the objects and the distance between them. A diagram of the two objects is shown below.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of these changes would *increase* the force of gravity between the two objects?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Two identical spherical objects separated by a distance are shown. The object on the left is labeled object 1, and the object on the right is labeled object 2.

choices.content: increasing the distance between the two objects

assessment_items.question_content:
Anaya is building a model of Earth’s orbit around the sun. First, she attaches a ball to one end of a string. Then, she holds the other end of the string and swings the ball in a circle, as shown below.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Complete the table to show what each part of Anaya’s model represents.**

Part of Anaya's model | Part of the Earth–sun system
:-: | :-:
hand | [[☃ dropdown 1]]
ball | [[☃ dropdown 2]]
string | [[☃ dropdown 3]]
hints: Let’s think about how the different parts of Anaya’s model relate to the Earth–sun system. To start, Anaya’s hand is at the center of the model. So, her hand must represent *the sun*, which is at the center of the Earth–sun system.
Next, the ball moves around her hand in a circular path. So, the ball must represent *Earth*, which orbits around the sun in a nearly circular path.
Finally, the string keeps the ball connected to Anaya’s hand. So, the string must represent *the force of gravity*, which keeps Earth in orbit around the sun.

So, the completed table looks like this:

Part of Anaya's model | Part of the Earth–sun system
:-: | :-:
hand | the sun
ball | Earth
string | the force of gravity

options.alt: A diagram shows a ball moving around a hand in a circular path. The ball and the hand are connected by a string.

assessment_items.question_content:
Omar is building a model of Earth’s orbit around the sun. First, he attaches a ball to one end of a string. Then, he holds the other end of the string and swings the ball in a circle, as shown below.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Complete the statement.**

In Omar’s model, his hand represents [[☃ dropdown 1]], the ball represents [[☃ dropdown 2]], and the string represents [[☃ dropdown 3]].
hints: Let’s think about how the different parts of Omar’s model relate to the Earth–sun system. To start, Omar’s hand is at the center of the model. So, his hand must represent *the sun*, which is at the center of the Earth–sun system.
Next, the ball moves around Omar's hand in a circular path. So, the ball must represent *Earth*, which orbits around the sun in a nearly circular path.
Finally, the string keeps the ball connected to Omar’s hand. So, the string must represent *the force of gravity*, which keeps Earth in orbit around the sun.
So, in Omar’s model, his hand represents the sun, the ball represents Earth, and the string represents the force of gravity.

options.alt: A diagram shows a ball moving around a hand in a circular path. The ball and the hand are connected by a string.
